text: FORDOUN, a parish in the co. of Kincardine, 10 m. WSW of Stonehaven. Pop. in 1801, 2,203; in 1841, 2,342.
FORDSTOWN, a hamlet in the p. of Girley, co. Meath, 2½ m. N of Athboy. Pop. in 1831, 157.
FORDSVILLE, a village in Marion co., in the state of Missouri, U. S., 128 m. SSE of Jackson.

FORDWICH, a parish and village of England, in Kent, 2 m. ENE of Canterbury, situated on the river Stour, which is navigable by small vessels. The v. is a member of the port of Sandwich, and is governed by a mayor and jurats. Pop. 231.
FORDYCE, a parish in the co. and 10 m. W of Banff. Pop. in 1801, 2,747; in 1841, 3,442.
FORE, a village in the p. of Feighan, co. Westmeath. Pop. in 1831, 119.

FOREECARREAH, a river on the W coast of Africa, having its embouchure 3 m. E of Malatong island. Its entrance is above 2 m. wide; but its course is short, as a ridge of rocks crosses it near its mouth.

FORELAND (NORTH), a promontory forming the NE extremity of the co. of Kent. The lighthouse on this cape is in N lat. 51° 22′ 29", E long. 1° 26' 47". It is a fixed light, elevated 184 ft. above highwater.

FORELAND (South), a cape on the E coast of the co. of Kent, 16 m. S of the North F., between Dover and Deal. It is the nearest point in England to the coast of France, being within 21 m. of the opposite coast. Two beautiful lighthouses, bearing E by S and W by N, 1,347 ft. from each other, have been erected on this cape, in N lat. 51° 8' 26", E long. 1° 22' 22". The upper lighthouse consists of a massive tower,-externally octagon, internally circular,—the lantern of which is about 372 ft. above high water mark, the perpendicular height of the cliff on which it is situated being about 290 ft. The lantern is furnished with 264 mirrors, which are enclosed on the side opposite the sea by 6 lenses. These mirrors, casting multitudinous reflections on each other, afford a strong and brilliant light, being clearly visible on the opposite coast, throughout the Downs, Ramsgate, and even Margate and the greatest portion of the Isle of Thanet. The lamp, which is in the centre of the lantern, consists of a large socket containing 4 burners; and supplies itself with oil by means of clockwork machinery, which, while it pumps up the oil to the wick, also returns the surplus quantity to the reservoir; and, in case of any defect or want of supply, by a small hydraulic balance strikes a sharp tinkling bell as a warning to the keeper. The lantern consists of a cupola, the roof and sides of which are composed of neatly wrought iron frames, apparently light, but sufficiently strong to stand against the most ordinarily violent tempest. It is enclosed by 48 oblong panes of plate glass, from 21 ft. to 4 ft. long. Around the cupola, on the exterior, is a balcony, with a castellated parapet, from which, in clear weather, the eye ranges over a considerable distance beyond the bare outline of the opposite coast, and far up and down the Channel. Passing from the upper lighthouse, about a quarter of a mile easterly, is the lower one, standing on the verge of the cliff. The light here is 275 ft. above sea-level. Within the lantern are suspended, from copper branches, 15 argand lamps, each having a burner of rather large dimensions, with a concave reflector about 20 inches in diam.

FORELANDS (WEST, NORTH, and EAST), three remarkable steep cliffy points,—the two first on the W, and the last on the E side of Cook's inlet, or the W coast of North America. At the North F. the Russians have a factory.
FOREMARK, a parish in the co. and 61 m. SSW of Derby. Area 2,870 acres. Pop. in 1831, 221; in 1841, 212.
FORENZA, a city of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicata, 15 m. NE of Potenza, near the crest of the S. Apennines. Pop. 6,100.
FOREST, a township in the p. of Middleton, in Durham. Pop. 884. Lead-mines are wrought in the vicinity.

FOREST, a small island of Canada, at the mouth of Lake Ontario, between which and Grand island is a narrow channel.—Also a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Landrecies. Pop. 1,590. -Also a village of Belgium, in the prov. and 7 m. SE of Liege, on the Verdre.
FOREST (BLACK). See SCHWARZWALD.
FOREST (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Carvin. Pop. 1,117.
FORESTBURG, a township in Sullivan co., in New York, U. S., 120 m. SSW of Albany. Pop. 433.
FOREST-HILL, a parish in the co. and 5 m. NNE of Oxford. Area 650 acres. Pop. 149.
FOREST-LAKE, a township in Susquehanna co., in Pennsylvania, U.S. Pop. 606.

FOREST-TOWNS (THE), four towns of Suabia, in the Black-forest, formerly subject to Austria, but now included in the grand-duchy of Baden, circle of the Wiesen. They are Rheinfelden, Seckingen, Lauffenburg, and Waldshut.
FORESTER'S ISLAND, a small high island in the N. Pacific ocean, about 15 m. from the coast of Prince of Wales's archipelago, in N lat. 54° 50'.
FORESTERTON, a village of New Jersey, in Burlington county, 15 m. E of Philadelphia.

FORESTIER ISLANDS, a group off the NW coast of Australia, at the distance of from 1 to 3 m. from the coast. The central and largest island, called Depuch, in S lat. 20° 37′ 47", has an elevation of 514 ft.
FORET (LA), a port of France, in the dep. of Finistere, cant. of Fovesnant, on the Baie-di-la- Foret. Pop. 1,500.

FORFAR, a parish nearly in the centre of Forfarshire. Superficial area 16 sq. m. The surface, FOR as it all lies within the How of Angus, presents a level prospect to the eye. Pop., including the burgh, in 1801, 5,167; in 1831, 7,049; in 1841, 9,620.

FORFAR, a royal burgh, and the county-town of Forfarshire, is situated nearly in the centre of the p., 5 m. E by N of Glammis; 14 m. N of Dundee; and 56 m. by way of Cupar and Dundee, from Edinburgh. The town occupies the bottom of a sort of basin, the immediately circumjacent ground sloping gently toward it on almost every side, but stands high in comparison to the general level of the country. The streets, though generally irregular in outline, are well-built, and of modern and comfortable appearance. In the S wing of the town is situated the F. academy. In Castle-street stands a handsome suite of county-buildings, of recent erection. The parishchurch, the new church of St. James, and the Episcopal chapel, are all creditable structures, and important features in the burghal landscape. The town has an excellent subscription news-room and library. Its chief trade is the weaving of osnaburghs and coarse linens. Hardly any factory work is done; but, in 1838, 2,569 hand-looms were employed on various common linen fabrics. Another important manufacture, is the making of a particular kind of shoes, well-adapted for a Highland district. During the years 1827-1831, the average annual revenue was £1,715. In 1839-40, the revenue amounted to £1,558 13s. 4d. F. unites with Arbroath, Montrose, Brechin, and Inverbervie, in sending a representative to parliament. Parliamentary constituency in 1840, 280; in 1848, 243. Pop. of the burgh, in 1831, exclusive of the landward part of the parish, 6,899; in 1841, 8,362.

FORFARSHIRE, or Angus, a county on the E coast of Scotland, stretching between the North Esk and the frith of Tay. It is bounded on the NW and N by Aberdeenshire; on the NE by Kincardineshire; on the E and SE by the German ocean; on the S by the frith of Tay; and on the SW and W by Perthshire. Its form is very nearly circular. The co. lies between latitude 56° 27' and 56° 57' N. Its medium extent, from N to S, is <math>28\frac{1}{2}</math> m.; from E to W, 29 m.; and its superficial area is 831 sq. m., or 532,243 acres. General features.] The co. consists of four parallel and very distinctively marked districts,—the Grampian, the Strathmore, the Sidlaw, and the Maritime. The Grampian district forms the NW division, and includes about two-fifths of the superficial area. Like the rest of the range, the Grampian mountains here run from SW to NE, forming the barrier between the Highlands and the Lowlands of Scotland. The portions of them included in F, are called the Binchinnin mountains; and, viewed in the group, are far from possessing either the grandeur of the Alpine districts of the W, or the picturesqueness and beauty of the highlands of the S. —The Strathmore district of F. is part of the great valley of that name, and stretches from the W boundary of the p. of Kettins, away NE through the whole co., to the lower part of the N. Esk. This district is called 'the How' or 'Hollow of Angus;' and is 33 m. long, and from 4 to 6 m. broad. The geological formation is that of old red sandstone; and it is intersected by numerous longitudinal ridges, some of which rise 200 or 300 ft. above the adjacent valleys.—The Sidlaw district of F. derives its distinctive features from the Sidlaw hills. These hills, composed of old red sandstone accompanied by trap, and overspread with an impervious boulder formation, are a continuation or offshoot of a range which runs parallel to Strathmore or the Grampians, from the hill of Kinnoul near Perth, to the NE extremity of Kincardineshire. They lift several of their summits upwards of 1,400 ft. above the level of the sea; and in some places are covered with stunted heath, while, in others, they are cultivated to the top. The Sidlaw district terminates at Red-head, a promontory on the coast, between Arbroath and Montrose; and measures about 21 m. in length, and from 3 to 6 m. in breadth. The maritime district of F. extends from the Tay and the limits of Liff and Lundy on the S, to near the mouth of the North Esk on the N. In extreme length, it measures upwards of 27 m.; in breadth, it varies from about 3 m. to upwards of 8 m. This district is, with a few exceptions, fertile, and highly cultivated. Excepting a few rounded jutting hills-some of which are designated by the Gaelic name of duns—its surface slopes gently to the frith of Tay on the S, and the German ocean on the E. At Broughty-ferry, where the frith of Tay is very much contracted, an extensive tract of links or sandy downs commences. In many places these downs evince, by their exhibiting extensive beds of marine shells, at heights varying from 20 to 40 ft., that they were at one period covered with the sea. Rivers.] No waters enter F. from the conterminous cos.; and only inconsiderable rills at two points, come down thence upon waters which form its boundary-line. All its waters, with the exception of the Isla and its tributaries which run into Perthshire to join the Tay, have their termination also within its limits, or at its boundaries. The principal streams, in consequence, are not of the class which the usage of Scotland dignifies with the name of rivers, but belong to the more humble class of 'waters.' The most northerly is the North Esk, whose principal tributaries are West water and Cruick water, both on its r. bank, and which forms, for a considerable distance before entering the sea, the NE boundaryline of the co. The next is the South Esk, which traverses the whole breadth of the co. from the highest range of the Grampians to the sea at Montrose, and whose principal tributaries are the Prosen on its r. bank, and the Neran on its 1. The Lunan rises near the centre of the county, and flows E to the sea at the point of division between the parishes of Lunan and Inverkeiler. Of a number of other streams which flow toward the German ocean or the frith of Tay, all, with the exception of the Dighty, are very inconsiderable, rarely having a course of more than 8 or 9 m. The Isla rises, like the two Esks, in the Grampians, but flows in general S, forms, for a number of miles, the W boundary-line, and through its own immediate tributaries and those of the Dean, which joins it immediately after entering Perthshire, drains the waters of about one-sixth of the co. away toward a junction with the Tay 10 m. above Perth.—The lakes of F. are all small—in no case much upwards of 1 m. in length-and are chiefly Lochlee, Lentrathen, Rescobie, and Forfar lochs, in the ps. of their respective names, and Balgavies loch in the p. of Aberlemno. The Tay, though it expands into an estuary 12 m. before touching the co., and cannot, while it washes its shores, be considered as a river, is greatly more valuable to F. than all its interior waters. From the mouth of the Tay to near Westhaven, the coast on the German ocean is sandy, and thence NE to near Arbroath, it cannot safely be approached on account of low, and, in many cases, sunk rocks. About 103 m. SE of the centre of this perilous part the Bell-rock lighthouse lifts its fine form above the bosom of the ocean. A mile NE of Arbroath the coast becomes bold and rocky, breaking down in perpendicular precipices, and, in many places, perforated at the base with long deep caverns, whose floors are boisterously rocky promontory, upwards of 200 ft. perpendicular, terminates this bold section of coast. Minerals.] F. is not remarkable for its minerals. Many searches have been made in the SW district, but without success, for coal. The manufacturing and most populous parts of the co. are wholly dependent for their fuel upon the collieries of Fife and Newcastle.—Limestone occurs in the Grampian, the Sidlaw, and the Maritime districts. That, among the Grampians, is what mineralogists call mountainlimestone. Several veins of rhomboidal spar of lime intersect the sandstone strata of the Sidlaws; and is wrought, though to only a small extent, in various places in the district. Sandstone abounds in all the districts except the Grampian. Much of it is red, and incapable of being cut with the chisel. In several of the Sidlaw ps. it occurs in strata of various thickness, some of them only from half-an-inch to an inch, which are cut into plates for roofing, and flags for paving. The strata are coated with scales of mica or tale, of a greyish-blue colour, and, in consequence, are easily separated. The most extensive range is in the p. of Carmylie, and along the SE declivity of the Sidlaw hills, and is worked in various extensive quarries. The strata here are of a very fine grain, white in colour, or with a slight tendency to blue or green, and are quarried or carved into columns, lintels, grave-stones, steps for stairs, and especially paving-flags of from 3 to 6 inches in thickness, which are shipped in large quantities at Arbroath. Lead, of the species called galena, black in colour, and metallically lustrous, occurs in various localities. An iron mine in the lower part of the parish of Edzel was for some time worked, but has long been abandoned. Shell-marl, formed from the exuviæ of several kinds of fresh water shell-fish, and greatly enriching to the country as a manure, abounds in various parts of Strathmore, or in contiguous lakes and swamps. Vast masses of jasper, varying in colour from a bright yellow to a deep red, and capable of being cut and finely polished into ornamental trinkets, are immersed in mica schistose rocks at the mouth of Glen Esk, and at the bridge of Cortachie, where the South Esk issues from among the Grampians.—Chalybeate springs, of important medicinal quality, well up in numerous places; but those chiefly resorted to are one near Montrose, three west of Arbroath, and one in the parish of Dunnichen. Soil and Agriculture.] The general colour of the soils of F. is red, of various intensity, inclining often to brown, or dark brown, or black. The moist soils are, in all cases, darker than the dry. On the uplands of the Grampians, a thin stratum of moorish earth generally covers the surface, over a whitish retentive clay, but frequently perforated by jutting rocks. In the glens of the Grampians, the secondary or alluvial soils are generally much mixed with sand. In 'the How of Angus,' the soils are all alluvial; but, only in the minority of instances, fertile. The mode of cropping, in the lower parts of the co., is similar to that of the more southern parts of Scotland; but, in the upland districts, does not, in general, admit a rotation of wheat. Swedish turnips and mangelworzel, however, have been scantily introduced. The grazing and the feeding of cattle are prosecuted, throughout the co., to a much greater extent than the rearing of them.—The entire extent of plantation cannot be less than 28,000 acres,equal to one-sixteenth part of the area of the whole co. The largest forest is that of Monroman moor, distributed among the ps. of Brechin, Farnell, Aberlemno, Guthrie, Kirkden, and Kinnel. Manufactures. F. is the chief seat of the coarse washed by the billows of the sea. The Red-head, a linen manufactures of Scotland, and conducts a very extensive commerce in fabrics made up from foreign flax and hemp. In the large towns the spinning of yarn in large mills, and the working of canvass, broad-sheetings, bagging, and other heavy fabrics, in factories, are conducted on a vast scale; and in the smaller towns, and the villages, the manufacture of osnaburghs, dowlas, and common sheetings, employs an enormous number of hand-looms. Of 4,000 powerlooms employed in Scotland on coarse linen fabrics, greatly the larger proportion are in the towns of Angus. A fair idea of the manufactures of the county will be formed by glancing at those of the towns, Dundee, Arbroath, Forfar, Kirriemuir, Montrose, and Brechin, in which—especially in Dundee -they are concentrated. In all the villages and hamlets the principal trade is the weaving of the prepared materials into cloth, and the purifying of them by bleaching.—Excepting roads which run up Glen Isla, Glen Esk, Glen-Lethnot, and Glen Mark, the Grampian district is almost wholly unprovided with facilities of communication; but the other districts of the co., for the most part, abound in roads, and, as to either their number or their quality, are not behind any portion of Scotland. Lines of railway run from Dundee NW to Newtyle, N to Forfar, and NE to Arbroath. Statistics.] F. contains 5 royal burghs, Dundee, Arbroath, Montrose, Forfar, and Brechin, and the towns of Kirriemuir, Glammis, Cupar-Angus, Broughty Ferry, Letham, and Douglastown. It comprehends 56 quoad civilia parishes, and unites with Mearns in giving name to a synod. The number of its parochial schools is 53, conducted by 60 teachers, and attended by a maximum of 3,386 scholars; and of its non-parochial schools, 223, conducted by 255 teachers, and attended by a maximum of 6,936 scholars. The co. returns a member to parliament. Constituency in 1840, 2,056; in 1848, 2,649. The assessed property in 1815 was £361,241; in 1842-3, £502,841, of which £312,400 was from land. Pop. in 1801, 97,127; in 1811, 107,264; in 1821, 113,430; in 1831, 139,606. The pop., in 1831, was distributed into 1,089 occupiers of land, employing labourers; 1,099 occupiers of land not employing labourers; 4,466 labourers; 3,721 labourers not agricultural; 8,574 manufacturing operatives; 9,760 persons employed in retail trades and handicrafts; 1,897 capitalists; 382 male servants, and 5,889 female servants. The total number of families, in 1831, was 31,730; and of inhabited houses 19,597. Pop. in 1841, 170,520, of whom 131,774 were natives of the co.—The number of poor on the roll in 1847 was 4,050.

FORG, a district and town of Persia, in the prov. of Laristan, 170 m. SE of Shiraz, at the foot of the Jebel-Abad. Pop. 2,000. It is enclosed by a mud wall and ditch, and contains a fortress which forms the residence of the khan, a caravansera, and about 200 houses. The town is abundantly supplied with water from an adjacent reservoir. The surrounding district is extremely fertile, producing corn, rice, barley, and cotton, in great abundance, and affording pasturage to large herds of cattle.

FORGAN, a parish in Fifeshire, on the S side of the Tay, between that river and the parishes of Leuchars and Logie. Area about 5,000 acres, of which nearly 4,000 are under cultivation. Pop. in 1831, 1,090; in 1841, 1,219.
FORGANDENNY, a parish in Perthshire, bounded on the N by the Earn. Its greatest length is about 8 m., and its extreme breadth 3\frac{1}{2} m. Pop. in 1831, 885; in 1841, 796.
FORGARIA, a village of Austrian Lombardy, in the prov. and 17 m. NW of Udine, near the r. bank of the Tagliamento. Pop. 1,050. FOR

FORGES, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, arrond. and 33 m. S of Charleroy. Pop. 558. It has manufactories of pottery and of hosiery, and an extensive brewery, and is noted for its butter and cheese. It contains also lime-quarries and mines of iron.-Also a commune in the prov. of Liege, arrond. of Chevron. Pop. 66.—Also a commune in the same prov., in the arrond. of Marchin. Pop. 213.
FORGES (LES), a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, arrond. of Gesves. Pop. 201.
FORGES (MONTAGNE DES), a mountain of France, in the dep. of Correze, cant. of Ayen-Bas, 15 m. WNW of Brives.

FORGES, or Forges-Les-Eaux, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine- Inferieure, arrond. of Neuchâtel-en-Bray. The cant. comprises 21 com. Pop. in 1831, 12,371; in 1841, 12,362. The town is 11 m. SE of Neuchâtel-en. Bray. Pop. in 1841, 1,653. It is noted for its mineral waters and for its manufactories of porcelain and glass. Butter and cheese form also important articles of local produce. Fairs are held twice a-year.
FORGEUX (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Rhone, cant. of Tarare, 18 m. SW of Villefranche-sur-Saône, on the l. bank of the Trenchin. Pop. 2,047.
FORGLEN, a parish in Banffshire, bounded on the E and SE by the Deveron, by which it is separated from Aberdeenshire. Area about 12 sq. m. Pop. in 1831, 820; in 1841, 771.
FORGNEY, a parish in co. Longford, 13 m. ESE of Ballymahon. Area about 5,454 acres. Pop. in 1831, 2,241; in 1841, 2,193.

FORGUE, a parish in Aberdeenshire, bordered on the NE by the Deveron and Banffshire. It comprises an area of about 50 sq. m., and is intersected by two rivulets, the Forgue and Frendraught. Pop. 2,489.
FORI, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, prov. of Mikawa, 23 m. E of Okasaki.
FORIA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Principato Citra, district and 11 m. SSE of Il Vallo, cant. and 6 m. ESE of Pisciotta. Pop. 400.

FORIA, a canton and town of Naples, in the prov. and 25 m. WSW of Naples, district and 16 m. WSW of Pozzuoli, on the summit of an extinct volcano, on the W coast of the island of Ischia. Pop. 5,100. It is ill-built, but contains several handsome churches and a convent; and is protected by a square battlemented watch-tower. It possesses also a good port, and considerable trade. The immediate environs present numerous traces of destructive volcanic agency; but the surrounding country is extremely fertile, and is covered with vineyards, gardens, and pleasure-houses. The canton comprises the island of Vandolena, and about one half of that of Ischia. It is noted for its thermal springs, especially that of Cetara.

FORKED-DEER RIVER, a river of the state of Tennessee, U. S., formed by the confluence of several streams, which have their source in Carroll and Henderson counties, and unite in the co. of Dyer. It runs through the S part of Lake Wood, and throws itself into the Mississippi in Tipton co., after a course, to a great extent navigable, of about 100 m.
FORKED HEAD, a headland of Cape Breton, on the SE coast, ENE of Fourchou harbour, in N lat. 45° 42′, W long. 60° 15′.
FORKHILL, a parish and village in co. Armagh, 6 m. SSW of Newry. Area about 12,600 acres. Pop. in 1831, 6,978; in 1841, 8,128. The village is 63 m. SSW of Newry. Area 26 acres. Pop. in 1841, 217.

FORKS, a fort of British North America, in the district of Athabasca, at the confluence of the Washa- Cummow or Clearwater river with the Athabasca. -Also a township of Northampton co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., near the confluence of the Delaware and Lehigh rivers, drained by Bushkill creek and its tributaries. It has a level surface, and its soil, consisting of calcareous loam, is generally fertile. Pop. in 1840, 2,166.—Also a township in Lycoming co., in the same state. Pop. 224.

FORLI, a legazione or administrative province and town of the Pontifical states. The leg. comprises an area of 550 geog. sq. m.; and is bounded on the NW and N by the leg. of Ravenna; on the E by the Adriatic; on the S by the leg. of Urbino and Pesaro, and the little republic of San-Marino; and on the W by Tuscany; and contains 3 distretto-governativo, viz.: Forli, Cesena, and Rimini. Pop. in 1829, 189,692; in 1833, 194,399. The surface towards the N is level and marshy, and the climate unhealthy. Towards the centre the heat is excessive, and earthquakes are of frequent occurrence. The principal rivers are the Marecchia, Savio, Ronco, Rabbi, and Montoni. The two latter unite near the capital. The soil is fertile, producing in great abundance grain, wine, oil, fruit, hemp, madder, saffron, anise, cummin, and silk. The manufacturing industry of this district is more active than that of any other portion of the Pontifical states. Forli, the cap. of the above leg., is situated in a fertile plain, near the confluence of the Rabbi with the Montone, 15 m. SW of Ravenna; 40 m. ESE of Bologna; and 176 m. NNW of Rome; in N lat. 44° 13' 4", E long. 7° 22' 10". Pop. in 1829, 14,734; in 1832, 16,000. It is surrounded by old walls, and has a garrison, but possesses little capability of defence. It is well-built, its streets are straight and spacious, and it has a handsome square. The principal edifices are the town-house—the large hall of which is adorned with paintings by Raphael—the cathedral, and the chapel of Santa-Maria, both also containing numerous works of art. There are besides numerous churches and convents, a college with a public library, and a savings' bank. The chief branches of local industry consist in silk-spinning, the manufacture of wax-cloth, oil, and saltpetre, and the refining of the produce of the adjacent sulphur-mines. Grain, wine, oil, anise, and other local productions, form the principal articles of its trade. In 1797 this town was taken by the French.—Also a town of Naples, in the prov. of Sannio, district and 8 m. N of Isernia. Pop. 2,200.

FORLIMPOPOLI, a town of the Pontifical states, in the leg. and 3 m. ESE of Forli. Pop. in 1833, 4,191. It has a cathedral, 2 parish-churches, a theatre, and several convents, and is enclosed by a high wall, with two gates.—This town, the Forum Popilii of the ancients, was one of the Fora, on the Via Emilia, in which the Roman magistrates held their court of justice. In 700 it was destroyed by the Lombards, and again in 1370 by order of Gregory XI. It has since been rebuilt and fortified, but has never regained its former importance.

FORMAN, or Foremon, a summit of the Grampians, in the p. of Forgue, in Aberdeenshire, which rises to the height of above 1,000 ft. above the level of the Deveron, which runs at its base. It is covered on all sides except the SE with wood.
FORMANTINE, formerly a district of Aberdeenshire, which comprised an area of 280 sq. m., bordered by the Don, Ythan, and Deveron.
FORMAZZA, a village of the Sardinian states, in the dep. of Novara, on the Tosa river. Pop. 300.
FORMBY, a chapelry in the p. of Walton-on-the- Hill, Lancashire, 72 m. W of Ormskirk, on the Irish sea. Pop. 1,446.
FORMELLO, a large village of the Pontifical FOR states, on the r. of the Via Cassia, 15 m. NW of Rome. Pop. 510.

FORMENTERA, an island of Spain, in the group of the Balearic islands, prov. of Baleares, partido of Ibiza, from which it is separated on the N by a channel 4 m. in breadth. Its E point is in N lat. 38° 42′ 10″, E long. 1° 35′ 23″. Pop. 2,000. Its length from E to W is 12 m.; its medium breadth about 3 m. It has no streams, but is supplied with water by numerous wells, and produces corn, wine, and oil, in considerable quantities. It has quarries of freestone, and extensive salt-mines.—This island was anciently named Ophiusa, from the number of serpents with which it was infested. It was afterwards called Pityusa Minor. Its modern appellation is said to be derived from its fertility in corn.

FORMENTON (CAPE), a headland on the NE side of the island of Majorca, forming the extremity of a long narrow peninsula which encloses the bay of Pollenza on the N, in N lat. 39° 57', E long. 3° 15'. [Raper.]

FORMERIE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Oise, arrond. of Beauvais. The cant. comprises 23 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,595; in 1841, 10,419. The town is 24 m. NW of Beauvais, on the road from Rouen to Abbeville. Pop. 1,257. It has a manufactory of hosiery, and a considerable trade in grain, cattle, and woollen and cotton fabrics. FORMICA, FORMICA-DE-BURANO, OF ERCOLE ISLAND, a small island near the coast of Tuscany, to the E of Porto Ercole.
FORMICHE, a group of islets in the Mediterranean, 5 m. S of Capo-del-Fino, the SE extremity of the island of Elba.

FORMICHE (LE), two small islands near the W coast of Sicily, 5 m. E of the island of Levanzo, and 6 m. WSW of Trapani, in N lat. 38° 1', E long. 12° 25'. The more easterly of the islands has some fortifications, and possesses a tunny fishery.

FORMICHE-DI-GROSSETO, a group of small rocky islands near the coast of Tuscany, in the prov. of Sienna, 2 m. S of the embouchure of the Ombrone. The principal are Formicola, Giannuti, Giglio, and Monte-Christo.
FORMICOLA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Terra-di-Lavoro, district and 12 m. NNW of Caserte. Pop. 2,000.

FORMIGA, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, comarca of Rio-Grande, near a stream of the same name, an affluent of the Rio-Grande. Pop., chiefly agricultural, 2,000.—Also a village in the prov. of Goyaz, 90 m. ESE of Natividade.

FORMIGAS, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, comarca of Jequitinhonha, 240 m. NNE of Ouro-Preto, and 60 m. ENE of Minas-Novas. It contains about 200 houses, mostly earthbuilt, and tiled, and has a spacious square, in which the parish-church is situated. It has an active trade in cattle, hides, leather, and saltpetre.

FORMIGAS, or Ants, a range of black rocks in the Formigas bank, at the distance of 19 m. NE of Matos, the NE point of the island of Santa-Maria, in the Azores. They run N and S, and are about 800 yds. in length, and 150 in extreme breadth. The most southerly forms for the space of about 350 yds. a comparatively compact mass, with a small bay on the W. Its S extremity, which rises to the height of 27 ft. above low water springs, is in N lat. 37° 16' 14", W long. 27° 47' 6". The principal summit of the group, named Hermigon, is 35 ft. in height.
FORMIGINE, a town of the duchy of Modena, in the district and 7 m. SW of Modena, on a canal of the same name. Pop. 2,000. It has a large educational establishment. A fair is held here once a-year.
FORMIGNY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. of Trevieres, 11 m. WNW of Bayeux. Pop. 500. The English were here defeated by the French under Charles in 1450.
FORMOSA, called by the Chinese Tai-ouan, or

TEA-WAN, and by the natives KABOSKI and GADAVIA, an island of China, lying between 22° and 25° N lat., and between 3° and 5° E long. from Pekin, situated about 30 leagues from the coast of the prov. of Fo-kien, and administratively comprised in that prov., constituting with the Pung-hu islands a fu or department. It is above 180 geog. m. in length from N to S, and nearly 80 geog. m. in breadth from E to W. La Perouse and Broughton place its SE point in N lat. 21° 53′ 30″, E long. 120° 57′. Ke-lung, its most N point, is in N lat. 25° 16', E long. 121° 4' 3". Though lying within so short a distance of the continent, it does not appear to have been familiarly known to the Chinese till 1430. It was merely visited in 1564 by the commander of one of their squadrons, when he subdued the little islands of Pung-hu, which lie about midway between it and China. In 1582, a Spanish ship sailing from Macao to Japan was wrecked on the coast, and brought the first accounts of the country to Europe. About the year 1620, a Japanese squadron left a party on the island, in the view of returning to effect its subjugation; but in the mean time, a Dutch ship having touched there in its course from Japan, it appeared to be so eligible a station for a commercial establishment, that they built Fort-Zealand on the SW coast, in 1634, and thus secured possession of the principal harbour; and erected factories at Ke-lung and Tenshui. In 1661 they were driven from the settlement by the Chinese pirate Tchin-tchi-kong, or Coshinga, who had made himself master of the W part, and held the sovereignty of the island during his life; but in 1682, his grandson, Tchin-ke-sun, submitted to the authority of the emperor Kang-hi, and the island since that period has been tributary to China. By a chain of mountains running from N to S it is divided into two parts, called the western and eastern provinces, -the latter of which is still occupied entirely by the aboriginal inhabitants, and the former contains the settlements of the Chinese. A governor, with a garrison of 16,000 men, resides constantly on the island. Tai-ouan, or Seng-Tyan-hu, in N lat. 23°, is the cap. and the seat of government. It is a large and populous place, and equal to several cities of the first class in China. The streets are remarkably straight, about 40 ft. broad, and cut each other at right angles. Some of them are a full league in length, but they are badly paved, and the houses meanly built of clay and bamboo, and thatched with straw. On account of the excessive heat of the sun, the streets are covered during 7 or 8 months of the year with awnings, which leave nothing to be seen but the shops on either side, in which various kinds of merchandise, ranged with great order and show, present a rich appearance to the eyes of the passenger. The town is said to be now surrounded with thick walls and a ditch. The old Dutch factory and church are still standing. The harbour is good, but its entrance is becoming daily more incumbered with sand. Its interior however is deep and extensive. The markets and shops contain every necessary of life and article of traffic; both foreign commodities, such as Chinese and Indian cloths, silk stuffs, porcelain, and European goods; and also the productions of the island, cotton, hemp, rice, sugar, salt, tobacco, dried stag's flesh, fruits of all kinds, and abundance of medicinal herbs. The commerce with China is entirely free; but those who wish to traffic with Siam, Cochin-china, or Japan, must obtain passports from a Chinese port. Small junks belonging to FORMOSA. Amoy go to all the W ports of the island, and either return loaded with rice, or go up to the N of China loaded with sugar. On the N point is the harbour of Ke-lung; on the NW coast is Tan-shui-kiang; and to the S of it Tan-shui-shing. The W province contains, besides the cap., and another garrison-town called Lo-kang, a number of smaller towns and populous villages, in which the Chinese permit none of the native inhabitants to reside along with them, except those who act in the capacity of slaves or domestics. It appears that the Chinese pop. of the island would increase with great rapidity, if the government would permit free emigration from other parts of the empire; but permission to new settlers is granted with great caution, and only upon the payment of a considerable sum for the proper passports. The reason of this is, that the Tartar emperors are always apprehensive of a revolt among their Chinese subjects; and the proximity of this island to China would render it a favourable field for the operations of malcontents. In fact the colonists of F. are said to be peculiarly unruly; and in 1788-9 a rebellion in this island cost China, it is computed, 100,000 men, and 2,000,000 of taels. The district, which the Chinese possess, consists of extensive and fertile plains, watered by numerous rivulets which flow from the mountains towards the E. The climate is temperate, except when the sun is vertical; and the air, serene and pure, is cooled even in the hot season with constant breezes. The existence of two volcanoes towards its E part, and of a boiling spring from which an inflammable gas issues, is reported. There is one mountain which is said to be loftier than any of those in Java; and in November and December has its summit sprinkled with snow. This would indicate an elevation exceeding 12,000 ft. It is generally called Ta-shan.—The soil is naturally productive. Besides most kinds of grain, which F. yields in abundance, the principal Indian fruits, such as oranges, bananas, cocoa-nuts, guavas, papaws, and pine-apples, are grown; and many of those which are common in Europe, particularly peaches, apricots, figs, grapes, pomegranates, and water-melons. Cinnamon, sugar, pepper, camphire, tobacco, are also among the ordinary productions of the country; and the mountains contain mines of gold, silver, copper, and sulphur. Ship-timber is found on the N side of the island. Sugar, rice, and camphor are exported in large quantities to China. The sugar is coarse. Rice is the principal production; and different kinds of pulse, corn, and Indian maize, are also grown. There is no good water to be found in the whole island; and strangers are said to suffer greatly from its bad qualities. There are few horses, sheep, goats, or hogs; but the inhabitants breed a great number of oxen, which they train for the purpose of riding; and, being early accustomed to this kind of service, they are said to go very securely and expeditiously. Domestic fowls, geese, and ducks, are reared in great plenty. The finest deer wander in large herds through the country. Tigers, leopards, monkeys, and every kind of game, abound in the forests; and the rivers furnish abundance of fish. The Indians inhabiting the W division are entirely subject to the Chinese governor, and pay a regular tribute in grain. They compose about 73 villages, most of which are found in the more N quarter of the province. In each v. is stationed a Chinese officer, whose duty it is to learn their language, and to superintend the collection of the impost; but these agents of the government often act in such a tyrannical manner as to occasion the defection of the inhabitants, who have sometimes been driven to unite themselves with the independent tribes in the E part of the island. Even those which are most submissive to their invaders still retain some of their ancient institutions; and in every v. three or four of the most intelligent old men are chosen as judges and rulers, who have the power of determining all disputes, and disobedience to whose decisions would be punished by perpetual banishment from the community. In the villages subject to China, and which are the most populous, there is a considerable degree of civilization; and the houses are built and furnished after the manner of the Chinese. The inhabitants are clothed with the skins of the stags which they kill in hunting; and wear on their heads caps of a cylindrical shape, made of palm-leaves, ornamented with a succession of crowns one above the other, and surmounted with plumes of feathers from the cock or pheasant. The dress of the women exactly resembles that of the men, except that their clothes are longer, and their heads more furnished with finery. The natives on the S and E coasts are still in a savage state. In colour they are darker than the Malays or the Javanese. They go naked with the exception of a belt round the waist descending to the knees. Their habitations are mere earthen huts, without any piece of furniture, and having only a kind of hearth in the centre upon which they dress their victuals. They are dirty in their manner of eating; and generally devour the flesh before it is half-dressed. Whatever they prepare is placed on a plain board or mat; having neither plates nor spoons, they make use of their fingers for conveying it to their mouths. They subsist chiefly upon rice and the produce of the chase. Their favourite weapons are lances, which they throw with dexterity and precision to the distance of 60 or 80 ft. They use also bows and arrows; and they run with such surprising swiftness that they can almost outstrip the fleetest greyhound. Those who are most swift and skilful in the chase, are distinguished by the privilege of having figures of flowers, trees, or animals punctured on their skin; the more ordinary decorations consist in wearing bracelets, or crowns made of shells and crystal, and in staining the teeth with a deep black colour. These people are represented as destitute of religious notions and worship, and as guided by the pretended predictions of a set of priestesses or female jugglers; but other accounts seem to imply that they are not without some ideas of the soul's immortality. There is, however, very little known respecting the E or S parts of the island and their inhabitants; indeed the accounts both of the Chinese and Dutch writers are filled with stories so obviously fabulous as to discredit their whole testimony. One of the most extraordinary of these histories-which was afterwards acknowledged by its author to be a mere romance—was published about the year 1710 by the noted Psalmanazar.—In consequence, it is supposed, of the sulphur mines in the mountains, which run across the middle of this island, F. is subject to frequent earthquakes; and in May, 1782, one of these shocks was attended with so tremendous a hurricane and swell of the sea as to threaten, for 12 hours, the total destruction of the Chinese settlements.—Grozier's Description of China, vol. i.—Collection of the Voyages of the Dutch East India Company, vol. v.—Du Halde's History of China, vol. ii.—M. Klaproth.—Gutzlaff.

FORMOSA, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, comarca of San-Francisco, on the Rio-Preto, an affluent of the Rio-Grande.—Also a lake in the prov. of Goyaz, on the Serra Itiquira.—Also a village in the prov. of Pernambuco, comarca of Rio- Formosa.—Also a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Pernambuco, which has its embouchure nearly opposite the islands of Santo-Aleixo, in S lat. 8° 39' 40".

FORMOSA, called also WARANG, an island of W. FOR Africa, the most northerly of the archipelago of the Bissagos. It is about 30 m. long and 18 m. broad. It is fertile and covered with trees, but wants water; and no European nation has attempted to form a settlement upon it.—Also a village of Sicily, 10 m. SE of Trapani.
FORMOSA BAY, a bay on the E coast of Africa, immediately N of Melinda, in S lat. 2° 45'. It receives a small river of the same name.
FORMOSA (CAPE), a cape on the coast of Malacca, 30 m. SE of Malacca.—Also a cape on the coast of W. Africa, which separates the Warri and Calabar districts, in N lat. 4° 28'.
FORMOSA (RIO). See BENIN.

FORMOSA (Rio), or BATU PAHAT, a river of the Malay peninsula, which rises near the sources of the Muar, and flows WSW in a winding course, through a low plain; receives a large tributary from the N; and falls into the sea 19 m. to the S of the embouchure of the Muar.
FORNALUTE, a modern town of Spain, in the prov. of Baleares, island of Palma, near the Sierrade-Alfavia. Pop. 1,146. It has a parish-church and a custom-house.
FORNAS, a small village of about 20 houses, upon the plain between the Xanthus and the hills of Patara, in Asia Minor, near the NE corner of the bay of Kalamaki.
FORNCETT (St. MARY), a parish of Norfolk, 3 m. WNW of St. Mary Stratton. Area 760 acres. Pop. 305.
FORNCETT (St. Peter), a parish in Norfolk, adjoining the above. Area 1,710 acres. Pop. 669.

FORNELLA, a seaport of the island of Minorca, 6 m. NNE of Mount-Toro, 12 m. NNW of Port- Mahon, in N lat. 40° 5'. It is capable of containing a large fleet of merchantmen, and is defended by three forts. In the neighbourhood is a fishing-village of the same name.
FORNELLI, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Sannio, 2 m. W of Isernia. Pop. 1,400.
FORNHAM (ALL SAINTS), a parish of Suffolk, 4 m. NW of Bury St. Edmunds. Area 2,200 acres. Pop. in 1831, 310; in 1841, 336.
FORNHAM (St. Genevieve), a parish adjoining the above. Area 760 acres. Pop. in 1831, 73; in 1841, 70.
FORNHAM (St. Martin), a parish adjacent to the above on the N. Area 1,230 acres. Pop. in 1831, 276; in 1841, 294.
FORNOLES, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 65 m. E of Saragossa. Pop. 564.
FORNOS, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 21 m. ESE of Vizeu, on the r. bank of the Mondego. Pop. 854.

FORNOVO, a village in the duchy of Parma, near the Taro, situated in a plain at the commencement of the Apennines, 8 m. WSW of Parma. Here Charles of France obtained a victory over the united Italian states in 1495.
FORO (LE GRAND), a village of Upper Guinea, in the state and 9 m. SE of Ardrah.
FOROMAN, a town of the island of Sumatra, near the W coast, 150 m. S of Acheen.
FORONDOY, a river of New Granada, in the dep. of Sulia, flowing NNW into Lake Maracaibo, after a course of 40 m.
FORRABURY, a parish of Cornwall, 5 m. NNW of Camelford. Area 400 acres. Pop. in 1841, 354.

FORRES, a small town and parish of Scotland, in the co. of Moray, situated on a rising ground near the bay of Findhorn, 10 m. WSW of Elgin. Pop. of p. in 1831, 3,895; in 1841, 3,711.—The town is a royal borough, and unites with Fortrose, Nairn, and Inverness, in returning a member to parliament # Pop. in 1841, 2,844. Electors in 1848, 153. The manufacture of linen yarn was formerly conducted to a considerable extent, but has declined for some time. Near Forres there is a remarkable stone obelisk, 25 ft. high, covered with ancient sculptures, which antiquaries have conjectured was erected in commemoration of a defeat of the Danes by Malcolm II. in 1,008.
FORRO, a village of Hungary, in the com. of Abujour, 9 m. NE of Szikszo.
FORRU, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 10 m. W of Isili, on the l. bank of the Uras. Pop. 1,326.
FORSBROOK, a township in the p. of Dilhorne, in Staffordshire, 21 m. WSW of Cheadle. Pop. in 1841, 843.
FORSCOTE, a parish in Somersetshire, 6 m. SSW of Bath. Area 580 acres. Pop. in 1831, 102; in 1841, 84.
FORSE, a river of Caithness, which runs N into the North sea, 6 m. W of Thurso.
FORSENOE, an island of Norway, in the bail. and 25 m. NNW of Stavanger, in N lat. 59° 18'.
FORST, a village of Baden, 2 m. NW of Bruchal. -Also a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the Lower Rhine, 6 m. W of Cologne.

FORSTE, or Forsta, a small town of Prussia, in Brandenburg, on a small island formed by the Neisse, 13 m. E of Cottbus. Pop. in 1837, 3,331. It has manufactories of pottery-ware, and linen and woollen cloth.
FORSTER'S BAY, a bay on the coast of Sandwich land, in S lat. 59°, between S. Thulé and Cape Bristol.—Cook.
FORSTER'S HARBOUR, a bay on the N coast of Labrador, on Hudson's strait, in N lat. 62° 18'.
FORSYTH, a co. in the N part of Georgia, U. S. Area 290 sq. m. Pop. 5,612.—Also the cap. of Monroe co., in Georgia, 55 m. W of Milledgeville .level. Also the cap. of Taney co., in Missouri.

FORSYTH ISLANDS, a group of four low isles off Point Bayley, on the N coast of Australia, in long. 6° 50' E of Port Essington. * FORT, a frequent prefix in British and American topography. For places not found below, see the second term of the compound.
FORTALEZA, a city of Brazil, the cap. of the prov. of Ceara, in S lat. 3° 42', W long. 38° 30'. Pop. 3,000.
FORTANA, one of the Bonin group, in the Pacific ocean, in N lat. 25°, E long. 143° 30'.
FORTANETE, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 34 m. ENE of Temel. Pop. 2,000.
FORT-ANN, a township in Washington co., in New York, U.S., on Wood creek, 68 m. N by E of Albany. Pop. 3,559.
FORT-AUGUSTUS. See Augustus.
FORT-BARRINGTON, a village of Georgia, U. S., on the E bank of the Alatamaha, 14 m. above Darien.

FORT-BROOKE, a military station of the United States, in E. Florida, in N lat. 27° 57', W long. 82° 35'. The mean temp. here, from 9 years' observation, was 72° 40'; the average mean temp. of winter, 62° 49'; of spring, 72° 21'; of summer, 80° 42'; of autumn, 74° 50'.
FORT-CHIPEWYAN. See CHIPPEWAYAN.
FORT-CONFIDENCE, a fortified port of the Hudson bay company, at the N extremity of Great Bear lake.

FORT-CONSTITUTION, a military station of the United States, in the township of Portsmouth, in New Hampshire, in N lat. 43° 4', W long. 70° 49'. The mean temp. here, on an average of 10 years, was 45° 04'; of winter, 25° 76'; of spring, 42° 80'; of summer, 63° 22': of autumn, 48° 41'. * FOR
FORT-COVINGTON, a township in Franklin co., in New York, U. S., 266 m. NNW of Albany, on Salmon river, which is navigable from this place to the St. Lawrence river, 5 m. below. Pop. 2,094.

FORT-DOUGLAS, a settlement in British N. America, at the confluence of the Red river and the Assiniboine, 40 m. S of Lake Winipeg. It was founded by Lord Selkirk. In the vicinity is a village consisting of a number of huts widely scattered along the margin of the river.
FORT-EDWARD, a township in Washington co., in New York, 49 m. N of Albany, on the E side of Hudson river. Pop. 1,726.

FORT-GEORGE, a fortress in the co. of Inverness, situated on the extremity of a low peninsula projecting from the S side of the Moray frith, 12 m. NE of Inverness. It is an irregular polygon of 6 bastions, constructed after the principles of Vauban, and mounted with 80 pieces of ordnance. The landfront, the only part of the works not washed by the sea, is defended by a ditch, ravelin, lunettes, covertway, and glacis. These communicate with the body of the place by draw-bridges. No neighbouring ground commands it; and its guns on the sea fronts, ranging from shore to shore of the frith, protect the entrance—about 1,600 yds. broad—of the bay leading to the Caledonian canal. Within the works are barracks for about 3,000 troops, quarters for the governor and staff, bomb-proof magazines, an extensive armoury, chapel, storehouses, hospital, brewery, bakehouse, artificers' workshops, &c. In two of the curtains are bomb-proof casements, into which the whole of the garrison off duty can retire. This fort was begun in 1746, and completed in 1764.
FORT-GRATIOT, a fort in St. Clair co., in Michigan, U. S., 60 m. NE of Detroit, in N lat. 42° 51', W long. 82° 53', at an alt. of 578 ft. above sea-
FORT-HAMILTON, a fortified post on the W end of Long island, U. S., adjoining the Narrows through which lies the main entrance to New York harbour. It mounts 70 guns.
FORT-HOWARD, a fortified port in Green bay, on Lake Michigan, U. S., in N lat. 44° 40', at an alt. of 600 ft. above sea-level.

FORT-LEAVENWORTH, a fortified port of the United States, on the W side of Missouri river, in N lat. 39° 20′, W long. 94° 52′, at an elevation of 912 ft. above sea-level. The mean annual temp. here is 52° 34'; that of winter, 27° 60'; of spring, 54° 39'; of summer, 74°; of autumn, 53° 38'.
FORT-LOUIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Bas-Rhin, cant. of Bischwiller. Pop. 532.
FORT-NASSAU, a fortress on the N side of the island of Timor.
FORT-ROYAL, a parish and town of Martinique, on the N coast of the bay of Cul-de-Sac-Royal. Pop. 11,300.
FORT (SAINT), a town of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, cant. of St. Genis, 14 m. W of Jonsac. Pop. 2,000.

FORT-SAINT-DAVID, a town of Hindostan, on the coast of the Carnatic, on the river Tripapolore, 15 m. SSW of Pondicherry. An English factory was first established here in 1686 or 1691, when a small territory was purchased from a Mahratta rajah. When Madras was captured by the French, in 1746, the English were besieged here, but made an effectual resistance. The town was taken, however, in 1785, by M. de Lally, and the fortifications destroyed.
FORT-SAINT-GEORGE. See MADRAS.

FORT-WILLIAM, a dismantled fortress in the co. of Inverness, at the E extremity of Loch Linnhe, and the commencement of Loch Eil. It was of a triangular form, regularly constructed, with two bastions mounting 20 12-pounders. This fortress was constructed originally during the protectorate of Cromwell, and on a large scale, having accommodation for 2,000 troops. It was then called the garrison of Inverlochy, from an ancient castle in the neighbourhood. The size was reduced, and the works strengthened in the time of William III., when its present name was given to it; and a neighbouring village received the name of Maryburgh.
FORT-WILLIAM. See CALCUTTA.

FORTEAU BAY, an extensive bay on the SE coast of Labrador, at the entrance of Belleisle strait. Its port is almost an open roadstead, exposed to the S and SE, but safe even with winds from those quarters, owing to its depth, and the protection it receives from the opposite coast of Newfoundland. It is surrounded by table-lands of sandstone covered with the usual moss, the dwarf-spruce, birch-bush, and mountain-ash. A salmon river of some size enters the sea at the head of the bay; seals, salmon, cod, and remarkably fine herrings, are plentiful. The fishings are carried on with considerable profit by five establishments; four connected with Jersey, Poole, and St. John's, are in the bay, and the fifth is planted upon the E point. Upwards of 400 people are employed by these various 'planters.' There are 9 fishing-stations between Red bay and Forteau, namely Carrol cove, East St. Modest, Black bay, West St. Modest, Capstan island, Lanc Diable, River head of Lanc-a-Loup, and Lanc-Amour; except at Lanc-a-Loup, all these stations are fished by colonial fishermen, who send their produce to St. John's by vessels from that port, and to the Jersey houses in Forteau-bay and Blanc Sablon. These small stations employ about 220 men, and average a catch of 25,000 quintals throughout the year. Cabbages and turnips are grown at every station for the summer-consumption of the inhabitants. Those who reside upon the coast during the winter shoot deer, partridges, ducks, geese, curlew and other wild fowl. They have stores of pork, flour, tea, and molasses, supplied by traders from Quebec, Halifax, and St. John's.
FORTESCUE BAY, a bay of S. America, in the straits of Magalhaen, between Cordes bay and Elizabeth bay.
FORTESSA, an island in the gulf of Venice, in N lat. 44° 40'.
FORTEVENTURA. See FUERTAVENTURA.
FORTEVIOT, a parish in the co. of Perth, on the banks of the Earn, adjoining Perth. Pop. in 1831, 624; in 1841, 638.

FORTH, a large and beautiful river intersecting two-thirds of the breadth of Scotland, and flowing E from Benlomond to the German sea. Its headwaters rise respectively in Stirlingshire and Perthshire, from points mutually distant, NE and SW, about 3 m. The S stream wells up on the N side of Benlomond; and, bearing the name of Deuchray water, after an entire course of 111 m. from its origin, forms a confluence with the N main head-water of the F. at a point 1 m. NE of Aberfoyle. The latter stream rises in two head-waters at spots a 1 m. and 1½ m. S of the celebrated Loch-Katrine. Both head-waters, without a previous confluence, and after brief courses, become lost in the expanse of Loch Con, which discharges its waters into a lochlet, called Dow-Loch, which in its turn discharges its superfluous waters into Loch Ard, whence the stream comes impetuously forth, and makes a magnificent leap over a rock nearly 30 ft. high; and a mile onward, after an entire though somewhat sinuous course of about 81 m., unites with the waters of the Duchray .- The united stream, during 5 m. of its course, is known simply as the Avendow or Black river. It flows softly along a beautiful and fertile valley called the Laggan, hemmed in on both sides by a fine amphitheatre of hills, with a narrow opening toward the SE; and through this opening the river glides away to commence its remarkable and characteristic serpentine evolutions in the champaign country which it henceforth traverses. After leaving Aberfoyle, it flows 21 m. SE; and having been joined by Kelly water, coming down upon it from the W, it takes a persevering direction, with the exception of its constant and involving and often spacious sinuosities, almost due E, and here assumes its proper name of the Forth. At the point of leaving Kincardine p., the F. receives the opulent tribute of the beautiful Teith. So capriciously, though gracefully, does the river move, that when about to receive the Teith, or about 13 m. above their confluence, it departs from its usual E direction, sends its windings away N, and eventually turns to the W of N; and, no sooner has it become united with the Teith than it suddenly debouches and resumes its prevailing course toward the E. About 300 yds. below the confluence, the river bounds over ledges of rock called 'the Cruives of Craigforth,' which stretch across its channel; and from this point downward, it is stemmed by the tide, and begins to bear aloft on its bosom the small craft of the inland navigator. For 11 m. onward it intersects a small wing of Stirlingshire; then receives from the north the important tribute of Allan Water, and then passes, a few hundred yards from its r. bank, the romantic town and castle of Stirling. Over the latter part of its course, or from a brief way after it enters the champaign country, and especially after passing the Cruives, it affords indications, in the flatness and composition of its immediate banks, of having, at a former period, expanded into an estuary and opened a path for the ingress of the sea much higher up than at present; and along this space it is dark-coloured in its waters, and solemn and sluggish in its current. Up to Stirling-bridge, the river is navigated by sailing-vessels of small burden, and by steam-boats plying between this point and Newhaven, - one of the ports of Edinburgh. From Stirling to Alloa the distance along the channel is 24 m., but in a direct line only 6 m. Along this distance it flows through the carse of Stirling and Falkirk, forming sweeps, curves, and crescents, and nearly complete circles and graceful departures of every sort, from the stern angle and the lank straight line, locally called 'links.' At a 1 m. above Tulliebody house, or 24 m. in a direct line above Alloa, the river has become a 4 m. broad, and receives from the N the large tribute of Devon-water; and, between that point and Alloa, it forms the beautiful islands, each about a m. in length, called Tulliebody and Alloa inches.—At Alloa, situated on its 1. bank, the F. relinquishes both its sinuosity of movement and its fresh water character; and, from this point, which is the extremity of its proper or productive navigation, whither vessels come up of 300 tons burden, it partakes the expansion and the other properties of a gradually widening and far-stretching estuary. At the village of Grangemouth, it receives the important waters of the Carron, and sends off inland, away to the W coast of Scotland, the Forth and Clyde canal, and again, at 2 m. distance, receives the tribute of the beautiful Avon. At this point, though 23 m. wide at high water, it is only I mile during the efflux of the tide; and all the way down from Alloa to this point, and several miles below it, presents alternate appearances of a brilliant expansion of water, between wide stretches of verdant landscape, and dreary lugubrious wastes of sands and sleeches, threaded along their centre by an imteresteresteresteresterentiller i in en elementemperaturation de proposition de la company de la company de la company de la company de la company de la company de la company de la company de la company de la company de la poverished, naked, and forsaken stream. About 9 m. below this, the F. suddenly contracts to the breadth of 1 m. and 3 furl., but is compressed to this breadth entirely by the protrusion, on its N side, of a peninsula; and having embosomed the islet of Beemer, the estuary, at the narrowest part of its contraction, is overlooked by the Linlithgowshire town of South Queensferry, and the Fifeshire village of North Queensferry, both pressing close upon its beach; and, in its centre, or at equal distances between them, it embosoms the islet of Inchgarvie. Below this strait it suddenly expands to the breadth of 3 m., sends off, behind North Queensferry, a small bay, at the head of which stands the town of Inverkeithing, and henceforth to the sea, a distance of 36 m., divides Fifeshire on the N, from the three Lothians, West, Mid, and East, on the S. At 4 m. below Inchgarvie are Cramond isle, 3 m. from the S shore; Inchcolm, with its attendant islets, Haystack and Carcraig, 3 m. from the N shore; and the little islet, Stone Wickray, in the middle of the channel. At 61 m. farther on, it runs abreast of Inchkeith, which stretches nearly 1 m. across the centre of its channel. At Leith the F. is 6 m. broad; and at the Bass, opposite the Anstruthers, and somewhat W of Fifeness, or the point where it fairly becomes lost in the ocean, it is 11 m. broad. About 4 m. E of Wemyss, on the N shore, it receives Leven water; and on the S shore it receives Almond water at Cramond, Leith water at Leith, Esk water at Musselburgh, and Tyne water 4 m. W of Dunbar. At 41 m. from the Fife coast, a little W of Fifeness, it embosoms May island; and, near the coast of East Lothian, it has, at various intervals, the islets of Eyebroughy, Fiddray, Lamb, Craig-Leith, Scarr, and the Bass. At intervals, on its N shore, E of West Wemyss, are the villages of East Wemyss, Buckhaven, Muthel, Inverleven, Leven, Largo, Elie, St. Monance, Pittenweem, Anstruther, and Crail; and, on its S shore, in East Lothian, are Prestonpans, Cockenzie, Port-Seaton, Aberlady, North Berwick, and Dunbar.—The frith of Forth is of great importance to navigation and commerce. Above Queensferry it is, in every part, one of the safest roadsteads in Britain. Inverkeithing bay, Burntisland roads, Leith roads, Elie roads, and various other localities, are places of safe anchorage. On the S side, the harbours are Grangemouth, Granton, Leith, Fisherrow, and Dunbar,-Granton being the best on the frith, and Leith the most frequented. On the N side, at most of the multitudinous towns and villages which sit upon the coast, are harbours superior, in general, to those on the S side, but less frequented; the one at Burntisland being the best on the frith except that of Granton. In early times the frith was regarded as of dangerous navigation; but, though shoally in various localities, and heaved up by sand banks, it is now-with the appliances of lighthouses on Inch-Keith and May island, and of accurately drawn and minute charts —so signally safe as to be hardly ever the scene of a shipwreck. The amount of trade on its waters was materially increased by the opening of the Forth and Clyde canal, and has been not a little augmented by the introduction and the progressively improving application of steam. Navigable to Stirling only for vessels of 80 or 100 tons, it might easily, by means of side-locks, or of a deep straight cut along the locality of its 'links,' be made to carry to that town vessels of most of the classes which enter it from the sea. On both shores from Borrowstounness downwards, are numerous salt-works; and along the coasts, as well as inland near the banks of the river, are vast repositories of coal, limestone, and iron-stone; and these, along with extensive and multitudinous fisheries, attract a very numerous resort of vessels. At Stirling, FOR Alloa, Kincardine, and numerous other places, are valuable fisheries of salmon. An annual shoal of herrings generally visits the frith, and in some years has yielded a prodigious produce; but its fish are esteemed decidedly inferior in quality to those of the western coasts of Scotland. At Cramond and Inchmickery were formerly vast beds of oysters; but from over-fishing they have been much exhausted; and they also yield a fish which in quality and size is generally inferior to that obtained in many places on the British coasts.—The Forth, it has been calculated, drains a superficies of 574 sq. m. Its entire length of course in a direct line is upwards of 90 m; but, including all the sinuosities for which it is so remarkable, it cannot be estimated at less than 170 m. The tides in the F. run variously, both in respect of time and velocity. This is caused partly by the formation of its shores, and partly by the obstruction of islands and shallows, and the meeting of currents; for instance, over the sands of Leith there is an apparent receding tide 2 hours before high water. On the N shore and in mid-channel the tides run equal in respect of duration, and at the rate of from 3 to 35 knots an hour. The ebb tide runs through the strait at Queensferry at 6 knots an hour.

FORTH AND CLYDE CANAL, a public work of national interest to Scotland, connecting, by a navigable communication, the friths of Forth and Clyde. The length of the work in all its parts is 384 m.; of the navigation direct from the Forth to the Clyde, 35 m.; of the side-branch to Port-Dundas, 24 m.; and of the continuation to the Monkland canal, 1 m. The number of locks on the E side of the island is 20, and on the W side 19; the difference being occasioned by the higher level of water in the Clyde at Bowling-bay than in Grange-burn or the Carron at Grangemouth. Each lock is 74 ft. long, and 20 ft. broad; and procures a rise of 8 ft. The greatest height of the canal is 141 ft.; its medium breadth at the surface, 56 ft.; and its medium breadth at the bottom, 27 ft. Its capacities admit vessels of 19 ft. beam, 68 ft. keel, and 8 ft. draft of water. It is crossed by 33 drawbridges, and passes over 10 considerable aqueducts, and upwards of 30 smaller ones or tunnels. The greatest aqueduct is a very magnificent one across the Kelvin at Maryhill, begun in June 1787, and finished in April 1791. It consists of 4 grand arches, is 83 ft. high, runs across a dell or valley 400 ft. wide, and was completed at a cost of £8,500. The canal has 6 reservoirs, covering about 400 acres, and containing upwards of 12,000 lockfuls of water; and means exist for more than doubling the supply. The navigation into the canal from the Forth runs half-a-mile up the river Carron, and thence a very brief distance up Grange-burn, and at low water is impracticable, leaving sailing craft of even a small size aground. The canal, lifted up from the tide at Grangemouth, is carried 24 m. SW on a straight line to Grahamston, and thence to Camelon, near Falkirk. At Lock 16, the canal sends off on its E side the comparatively recent and less spacious navigation to Edinburgh, called the Union canal. Over nearly the whole distance from Lock 16 to Port-Dundas, the level or course of the canal is overlooked or flanked with confined views; in some places it carries the eye a short way over cheerless morass and moorland; in others it discloses limited but not uninteresting hillscenery on the N; and in a few it ploughs its way between steep and wooded, though not high banks, which all but cheat a stranger into the conviction that he is sailing along a natural river. The side-branch to Port-Dundas somewhat abounds in sinuosities, and has several rapid and inconvenient turns, but on the whole has a direction due SE; and as it enters the basin, displays a little forest of masts high above the general level of Glasgow. From the point whence the side-branch diverges, the canal adopts a considerable change of course, and after crossing the Kelvin. and reaching a point a little within the limits of West Kilpatrick p., where it is only about a 1 m. distant from the Clyde, it is joined by a brief junction canal, opened in 1839 for the benefit of Paisley, from the Clyde at the influx of the Cart. Hence onward for 34 m. it follows the course of the Clyde in an undeviating direction to the N of W; and, with little harbour-accommodation, or no more than admits a brief line of two or three vessels, is somewhat ceremoniously let down into the Clyde at Bowling-bay. The original cost of the canal, including all expenditure up to the January succeeding the date of its completion, was £330,000. The tonnage dues imposed were, from sea to sea, 5s. 10d.
FORTH MOUNTAINS, an extra-parochial district of co. Wexford. Pop. in 1831, 1,102.
FORTHAMPTON, a parish in Gloucestershire, 2½ m. W of Tewkesbury, intersected by the Severn. Area 2,390 acres. Pop. in 1831, 459; in 1841, 460.
FORTHERLY (HIGH), a township in the p. of Bywell-St.-Peter's, 7 m. WSW of Hexham. Pop. in 1831, 105; in 1841, 106.
FORTHO, or Furtho, a parish of Northamptonshire, 7 m. WSW of Towcester, and near the Grand Junction canal. Area 480 acres. Pop. in 1831 and 1841, 16.

FORTIFIED ISLAND, or BASWARAJE, an island of the Indian ocean, near the Malabar coast, in the prov. of Canara, to the N of Onore bay, in N lat. 14° 17'. It is about a mile in circuit, and is covered with palm and cocoa-nut trees and plantains. It derives its name from the fortifications which were erected here by Tippu-Saib. It was taken from him by the British in 1792.

FORTINGAL, a large and important parish, occupying the chief part of the NW of Perthshire. It measures in extreme length about 40 m.; in extreme breadth upwards of 30 m.; in circumf. along the sinuosities of its boundary-line probably 130 m. or upwards; and in superficial area nearly 450,000 imperial acres. The whole p. lies among the Grampians, and is exceedingly mountainous, and in general broadly marked with the characteristic features of the Highlands. It is naturally and comprehensively divided into three portions, Rannoch, Glenlyon, and F. proper. The most remarkable of the isolated heights is Schichallion, on the S boundary. The p. has at its centre, along the base of the intersecting broad belt of mountains, Loch Rannoch, 12 m. long, and upwards of 1 m. in average breadth, overlooked by grand and magnificent scenery. It has also at its N limit 7 m. of Loch Ericht, which stretches away into Inverness-shire. The Grampian bed of limestone, ranging from Dumbartonshire to Aberdeenshire, passes along the E end of the p. Veins of marble of various hues, and variously clouded, occur in several localities. A very rich vein of lead-ore in Glenlyon was wrought for several years toward the close of last cent.; but, owing to some unexplained reason, it did not compensate the working, and was abandoned. Brilliant pebbles, spars, and rock-crystals, are occasionally found among the mountains. In the very small area of the p. which is arable—yet small only as compared with the vast aggregate of impracticable surface—agricultural improvement has been rapid, and achieved surprising results. A considerable forest of native fir, and an extensive one of birch, range along the district of Rannoch, and appear to be remnants of that great Caledonian forest which anciently covered N. Perthshire and the co. of Inverness, over mountain, glen, and morass, to the extent of more than 2,000 sq. m. Less than a cent. ago, or up to the year 1745, this district was in an utterly barbarous condition, under little legal restraint, and signalized, even among the lawless regions around it, for its deeds of violence. Pop. in 1801, 3,875; in 1831, 3,067; in 1841, 2,740. The quoad sacra p. of F. is only about 8 m. long, and as many broad; and consists of the vale of F., a small part of Glenlyon, and the detached district of Bolfracks.

FORTON, a township in the p. and 4 m. N of Garstang, Lancashire, intersected by the Lancaster and Preston railway and canal. Area 1,280 acres. Pop. in 1831, 662; in 1841, 679.—Also a tything in the p. of Chard, Somerset. Pop. 209.—Also a tything in the p. of Longparish, Southampton. Pop. 281.

FORTORE, or Fronto, a river of Naples, in the prov. of Capitanata, which has its source in the Apennines, 6 m. SE of Volturara; runs N; passes along the confines of the prov. of Sannio; receives thence the Fiumicello, a considerable stream; and bending ENE, throws itself into the Adriatic 17 m. ESE of Termoli.

FORTROSE, a royal burgh in the co. of Ross, and p. of Rosemarkie. It is situated on the N side of the Moray frith, nearly opposite to Fort-George, * FOS from which it is distant 2½ m., and 10½ m. NE of Inverness. It is composed of two towns, viz., Rosemarkie, and Chanonry or Fortrose, which are about a m. distant from each other, but have been politically united in one burgh by royal charter. Pop. 758. There is a good harbour at F., about 30 yds. square; three sides of it forming an extensive wharf. Spring-tides rise 14 ft. within it. There is a regular ferry to Fort-George from the extremity of a tongue of land called Chanonry-ness, which runs out between the two towns into the frith; but it is not much frequented. F. joins with the northern district of burghs in sending a member to parliament. Electors in 1848, 52.

FORTUNA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 16 m. NNE of Murcia, and partido of Cieza. Pop. 4,010. It has a parish-church, thermal springs and baths, and a custom-house; and possesses a manufactory of saltpetre.
FORTUNADE (SAINTE), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Corrèze, cant. and 4 m. S of Tulle. Pop. 1,961.
FORTUNAT (SAINT), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, cant. of La Voulte, 9 m. NNE of Privas, on the l. bank of the Erieux. Pop. 1,523.

FORTUNE BAY, an extensive indentation of the S coast of Newfoundland. It is formed on the S and E by a long and narrow peninsula, by which it is separated from Placentia bay, and bordered on the N by a district of the same name. It is from 65 to 70 m. in depth, and from 20 to 30 m. in breadth. The surrounding coast is bleak, rugged, and barren. The islands of St. Pierre and Miquelon lie off the mouth of this bay.
FORTUNE ISLAND, or GOOD FORTUNE ISLAND, a small island in the Indian sea, near the SW coast of the island of Sumatra, in S lat. 1° 35', E long. 90° 25′.
FORTUNE ISLAND, a small island near the N coast of the island of Celebes, in N lat. 0° 50'.
